expanded_description:
The Bank of Canada is the nation's central bank, responsible for monetary policy, financial stability, currency issuance, and payment system oversight. Founded in 1934, the Bank operates under the Bank of Canada Act and has a unique governance structure: the Governing Council (Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, and four Deputy Governors) sets monetary policy, while the Board of Directors (appointed by the Minister of Finance) oversees management. Unlike the Federal Reserve's floor system (ample reserves with IORB as the primary tool), the Bank of Canada operates a corridor system for implementing monetary policy, with a target for the overnight rate and a 50 basis point operating band. The Bank also acts as fiscal agent for the federal government, manages foreign exchange reserves, and promotes financial stability.

Understanding the Bank of Canada's structure is essential for analyzing how monetary policy is made in Canada, how the Bank implements its policy rate (the overnight rate target), and how it differs from other central banks (especially the Fed). The Bank's corridor system is a key feature: the overnight rate target is the midpoint of a 50 basis point band (e.g., 5.00% target with a band of 4.75% to 5.25%). The Bank uses open market operations (SPRAs and LLAs) to keep the overnight rate within the band. This contrasts with the Fed's floor system (IORB floor, ON RRP floor). The Bank also has a strong focus on inflation targeting (2% target since 1991), making it one of the most successful inflation-targeting central banks.

In this section, we will examine the Bank of Canada's governance structure (Governing Council, Board of Directors), its core functions (monetary policy, lender of last resort, currency issuance, payment systems, fiscal agent), its monetary policy implementation framework (corridor system, overnight rate target, open market operations), and its approach to financial stability. You will learn to compare the Bank's corridor system with the Fed's floor system, understand the role of the Governing Council in setting policy, and evaluate the Bank's inflation-targeting framework.

core_concept.principle:
The Bank of Canada is Canada's central bank, responsible for monetary policy, financial stability, currency issuance, and payment system oversight. It operates a corridor system for implementing monetary policy, with a target for the overnight rate and a 50 basis point operating band.

Governance Structure:

• Governing Council: Sets monetary policy. Comprises the Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, and four Deputy Governors.
• Board of Directors: Oversees management (not monetary policy). Comprises the Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, and 12 outside directors appointed by the Minister of Finance.
• Governor: Appointed by the Board (with Cabinet approval) for a 7-year term (renewable).
• The Bank is accountable to Parliament through the Minister of Finance.

Core Functions:

1. Monetary policy: Inflation targeting (2% target since 1991). Sets overnight rate target.
2. Lender of last resort: Provides liquidity to solvent but illiquid financial institutions.
3. Currency issuance: Designs, produces, and distributes bank notes.
4. Payment system oversight: Operates and oversees large-value payment systems (LVTS, Lynx).
5. Fiscal agent for the Government of Canada: Manages federal debt, auctions bonds, processes payments.
6. Foreign exchange reserves management: Manages Canada's official international reserves.

Corridor System (Monetary Policy Implementation):

• Overnight rate target: The Bank sets a target for the overnight interest rate (the rate at which banks lend reserves to each other).
• Operating band: The target ± 25 basis points (e.g., target 5.00%, band 4.75% to 5.25%).
• Deposit rate (floor): Interest paid on settlement balances (deposit accounts at the Bank). Set at the bottom of the band (e.g., 4.75%).
• Bank Rate (ceiling): Interest charged on overnight loans to financial institutions. Set at the top of the band (e.g., 5.25%).
• Open market operations: The Bank uses SPRAs (Sale and Repurchase Agreements) and LLAs (Liquidity Adjustment Auctions) to keep the overnight rate within the band.

Comparison: Bank of Canada (Corridor) vs. Federal Reserve (Floor):

| Feature | Bank of Canada (Corridor) | Federal Reserve (Floor) |
|---------|--------------------------|-------------------------|
| Operating framework | Corridor system (±25bp band) | Floor system (IORB floor, ON RRP floor) |
| Primary tool | Overnight rate target | IORB (interest on reserves) |
| Reserves | Scarce (pre-2020) / Ample (post-2020) | Ample (since 2008) |
| Open market operations | Active (SPRAs, LLAs) | Passive (repos only for liquidity) |
| Monetary policy independence | Yes (floating CAD) | Yes (floating USD) |

Inflation Targeting (since 1991):

• Target: 2% inflation (CPI), within a range of 1-3%.
• The Bank adjusts the overnight rate to achieve the target.
• Canada was one of the first countries to adopt inflation targeting (along with New Zealand).
• The framework has been successful: inflation has averaged 2% since 1991, and the economy has been stable.

Bank of Canada vs. Fed: Key Differences:

• Implementation: Corridor (BoC) vs. Floor (Fed).
• Reserves: BoC historically maintained scarce reserves (now ample); Fed has ample reserves.
• Governance: BoC's Governing Council (6 members) vs. Fed's FOMC (12 members).
• Mandate: BoC has single mandate (price stability); Fed has dual mandate (price stability + maximum employment).
• Exchange rate: Both floating, but CAD is more heavily influenced by commodity prices.
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine the Bank of Canada as the conductor of Canada's monetary orchestra. The Governing Council (Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, and four Deputy Governors) meets eight times per year to set the overnight rate target. This is the key interest rate that influences all other rates in the economy (mortgages, business loans, credit cards). The Bank then implements this target using the corridor system.

The corridor system has three key rates: the overnight rate target (midpoint), the deposit rate (floor), and the Bank Rate (ceiling). For example, if the target is 5.00%, the deposit rate is 4.75% and the Bank Rate is 5.25%. Banks can borrow overnight from the Bank at the Bank Rate (ceiling) and can deposit excess reserves at the Bank at the deposit rate (floor). The overnight rate (the rate at which banks lend to each other) should trade within this 50 basis point band. The Bank uses open market operations (SPRAs and LLAs) to keep the overnight rate near the target.

This contrasts with the Federal Reserve's floor system. The Fed has ample reserves (since QE) and uses IORB (interest on reserves) as a floor. Banks will not lend reserves below IORB because they can earn IORB risk-free. The Fed's discount window acts as a ceiling (rarely used). The Fed does not need to conduct daily open market operations; the floor does the work. The Bank of Canada's corridor system requires more active management but gives the Bank more control over the overnight rate.

The Bank of Canada has a single mandate: price stability (2% inflation target). Since adopting inflation targeting in 1991, the Bank has successfully kept inflation around 2%. This has made Canada a model for other central banks. The Fed has a dual mandate (price stability + maximum employment).

For Canadians, the Bank's decisions affect mortgage rates (variable-rate mortgages are common in Canada), the exchange rate (CAD/USD), and the overall health of the economy. The Bank's independence from political pressure is crucial for its credibility. The Governor is appointed for a 7-year term, insulating him or her from electoral cycles.

image.alt: Organizational chart of the Bank of Canada: Governing Council (Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, 4 Deputy Governors) sets monetary policy; Board of Directors (Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, 12 outside directors) oversees management.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.1: The Bank of Canada's governance structure. The Governing Council sets monetary policy; the Board of Directors oversees management and administration.

sections.introduction:
The Bank of Canada is the nation's central bank, responsible for monetary policy, financial stability, currency issuance, and payment system oversight. Founded in 1934, the Bank operates under the Bank of Canada Act and has a unique governance structure: the Governing Council (Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, and four Deputy Governors) sets monetary policy, while the Board of Directors (appointed by the Minister of Finance) oversees management. Unlike the Federal Reserve's floor system (ample reserves with IORB as the primary tool), the Bank of Canada operates a corridor system for implementing monetary policy, with a target for the overnight rate and a 50 basis point operating band. The Bank also acts as fiscal agent for the federal government, manages foreign exchange reserves, and promotes financial stability.

Understanding the Bank of Canada's structure is essential for analyzing how monetary policy is made in Canada, how the Bank implements its policy rate (the overnight rate target), and how it differs from other central banks (especially the Fed). The Bank's corridor system is a key feature: the overnight rate target is the midpoint of a 50 basis point band (e.g., 5.00% target with a band of 4.75% to 5.25%). The Bank uses open market operations (SPRAs and LLAs) to keep the overnight rate within the band. This contrasts with the Fed's floor system (IORB floor, ON RRP floor). The Bank also has a strong focus on inflation targeting (2% target since 1991), making it one of the most successful inflation-targeting central banks.

In this section, we will examine the Bank of Canada's governance structure (Governing Council, Board of Directors), its core functions (monetary policy, lender of last resort, currency issuance, payment systems, fiscal agent), its monetary policy implementation framework (corridor system, overnight rate target, open market operations), and its approach to financial stability.

introduction_image.alt: Photo of the Bank of Canada building in Ottawa; timeline of key dates (1934 founded, 1991 inflation targeting, 2020 COVID response).
introduction_image.caption: Figure 6.5.2: The Bank of Canada headquarters in Ottawa. Founded in 1934, the Bank has been an inflation-targeting central bank since 1991.

key_concept.definition:
Bank of Canada: Canada's central bank, responsible for monetary policy, financial stability, currency issuance, and payment system oversight.

Governance Structure:

• Governing Council: Sets monetary policy. Comprises the Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, and four Deputy Governors.
• Board of Directors: Oversees management (not monetary policy). Comprises the Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, and 12 outside directors appointed by the Minister of Finance.
• Governor: Appointed by the Board (with Cabinet approval) for a 7-year term (renewable).

Core Functions:

1. Monetary policy: Inflation targeting (2% target since 1991). Sets overnight rate target.
2. Lender of last resort: Provides liquidity to solvent but illiquid financial institutions.
3. Currency issuance: Designs, produces, and distributes bank notes.
4. Payment system oversight: Operates and oversees large-value payment systems (LVTS, Lynx).
5. Fiscal agent for the Government of Canada: Manages federal debt, auctions bonds, processes payments.
6. Foreign exchange reserves management: Manages Canada's official international reserves.

Corridor System (Monetary Policy Implementation):

• Overnight rate target: The Bank sets a target for the overnight interest rate.
• Operating band: The target ± 25 basis points.
• Deposit rate (floor): Interest paid on settlement balances (bottom of band).
• Bank Rate (ceiling): Interest charged on overnight loans to financial institutions (top of band).
• Open market operations: SPRAs (Sale and Repurchase Agreements) and LLAs (Liquidity Adjustment Auctions).

Inflation Targeting (since 1991):

• Target: 2% inflation (CPI), within a range of 1-3%.
• The Bank adjusts the overnight rate to achieve the target.
• Canada was one of the first countries to adopt inflation targeting.

Comparison: Bank of Canada (Corridor) vs. Federal Reserve (Floor):

• BoC: Corridor system, active OMOs, historically scarce reserves.
• Fed: Floor system (IORB), passive OMOs, ample reserves.
• BoC: Single mandate (price stability). Fed: Dual mandate (price stability + employment).
• Both have floating exchange rates, but CAD is more commodity-sensitive.
key_concept.context: The Bank of Canada was founded in 1934 as a private institution, becoming a Crown corporation in 1938. It adopted inflation targeting in 1991, becoming a pioneer (along with New Zealand). The Bank's corridor system has evolved: before 1999, the Bank used a target for the overnight rate with a 50bp band but no deposit facility; the deposit facility (floor) was introduced in 1999. The Bank has maintained a floating exchange rate since 1970 (with a brief pegged period in 1998-1999). The Bank's response to the 2008 crisis (rate cuts, liquidity facilities) and the 2020 pandemic (rate cuts to 0.25%, QE) has been praised. The Bank's independence from political pressure is enshrined in the Bank of Canada Act, but the Governor testifies to Parliament regularly.

image.alt: Graph: Overnight rate trading within corridor (deposit rate floor, Bank Rate ceiling); target rate midpoint; SPRAs and LLAs keep rate near target.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.3: The Bank of Canada's corridor system. The overnight rate target is the midpoint of a 50 basis point band. The deposit rate is the floor; the Bank Rate is the ceiling. Open market operations keep the overnight rate near the target.

classifications.type: Governance: Governing Council vs. Board of Directors
classifications.value: The Governing Council sets monetary policy; the Board of Directors oversees management and administration
characteristics: Governing Council: Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, 4 Deputy Governors (6 members total).
Governing Council meets 8 times per year to set the overnight rate target.
Board of Directors: Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, 12 outside directors appointed by Minister of Finance.
Board oversees management, approves budget, appoints auditors, but does not set monetary policy.
Governor appointed for 7-year term (renewable) by the Board with Cabinet approval.
The Bank is accountable to Parliament through the Minister of Finance (regular testimony).
classifications.behavior:
The Bank of Canada's governance structure separates monetary policy (Governing Council) from administration (Board of Directors). This is similar to many central banks but differs from the Fed, where the Board of Governors (7 members) sets policy alongside regional bank presidents. The Governing Council's small size (6 members) allows for efficient decision-making, unlike the Fed's larger FOMC (12 voters).

The Governor is the chief executive officer and primary spokesperson. The Governor's 7-year term insulates him or her from political pressure; the term is renewable, but no Governor has served more than two terms (the typical term is 7 years).

The Board of Directors includes the Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, and 12 outside directors from across Canada (representing regions). The Board ensures the Bank is well-managed but does not interfere with monetary policy. This separation is crucial for central bank independence.

The Bank is accountable to Parliament. The Governor testifies before the House of Commons Standing Committee on Finance twice per year (after each Monetary Policy Report). This transparency enhances credibility.

image.alt: Diagram: Governing Council (6 members) sets policy; Board of Directors (14 members) oversees management; Minister of Finance appoints directors.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.4: Bank of Canada governance structure. The Governing Council sets monetary policy; the Board of Directors oversees management; the Minister of Finance appoints directors but does not direct policy.

examples.explanation: The Governing Council meets 8 times per year. Staff present economic projections (Monetary Policy Report). The Council discusses the outlook and decides on the overnight rate target. The decision is announced at 10:00 AM ET. The Governor holds a press conference to explain the decision.

Tiff Macklem was appointed Governor in 2020 for a 7-year term. The Board of Directors (with Cabinet approval) made the appointment. Macklem had previously been Senior Deputy Governor (2010-2014). The appointment process is non-political, enhancing independence.

classifications.value: The Bank of Canada uses a corridor system (target ± 25bp); the Federal Reserve uses a floor system (IORB floor)
characteristics: BoC corridor: overnight rate target ± 25bp band (e.g., 4.75% floor, 5.00% target, 5.25% ceiling).
BoC uses active open market operations (SPRAs, LLAs) to keep the overnight rate near target.
Fed floor: IORB (interest on reserves) acts as a floor; ON RRP provides floor for non-banks.
Fed uses passive open market operations (reserves are ample; no fine-tuning needed).
BoC historically maintained scarce reserves (now ample due to QE).
The corridor system gives the Bank more control over the overnight rate but requires more active management.
classifications.behavior:
The Bank of Canada's corridor system is designed to keep the overnight rate (the rate at which banks lend reserves to each other) within a 50 basis point band around the target. The deposit rate (floor) is the interest the Bank pays on settlement balances; banks will not lend reserves below this rate. The Bank Rate (ceiling) is the interest the Bank charges on overnight loans; banks will not borrow above this rate. The overnight rate trades between these bounds.

The Bank uses open market operations to keep the overnight rate near the target. SPRAs (Sale and Repurchase Agreements) are used to add liquidity (when the overnight rate is above target). LLAs (Liquidity Adjustment Auctions) are used to drain liquidity (when the rate is below target). This active management contrasts with the Fed's floor system, where the IORB rate does most of the work and open market operations are minimal.

Before 2020, the Bank maintained scarce reserves (reserves just sufficient to meet demand). This required daily fine-tuning. After the 2020 pandemic, the Bank engaged in QE, creating ample reserves. The corridor system still works with ample reserves, but the Bank now uses a 'corridor with ample reserves' approach, similar to the Fed but with a narrower band.

For Canadians, the corridor system means that the overnight rate (and thus mortgage rates, prime rate) is predictable and stable. The Bank's active management ensures that the policy rate is transmitted effectively to the economy.

image.alt: Two graphs: Bank of Canada corridor (deposit rate floor, target, Bank Rate ceiling) vs. Federal Reserve floor (IORB floor, ON RRP floor, discount window ceiling).
image.caption: Figure 6.5.5: Comparison of Bank of Canada corridor system and Federal Reserve floor system. The BoC uses a 50bp band; the Fed uses IORB as a floor.

examples.explanation: In July 2023, the Bank raised the overnight rate target from 4.75% to 5.00%. The deposit rate rose from 4.50% to 4.75%; the Bank Rate rose from 5.00% to 5.25%. The Bank conducted SPRAs to keep the overnight rate near 5.00%. The hike transmitted to prime rates (Bank of Canada prime rate rose from 6.95% to 7.20%).

If the overnight rate is trading above the target (e.g., 5.10% when target is 5.00%), the Bank conducts a SPRA (Sale and Repurchase Agreement). The Bank sells government bonds to primary dealers, draining reserves, which pushes the overnight rate down. This fine-tuning keeps the rate near target.

classifications.value: The Bank of Canada has targeted 2% inflation since 1991, making it one of the most successful inflation-targeting central banks
characteristics: Target: 2% inflation (CPI), within a range of 1-3%.
The Bank adjusts the overnight rate to achieve the target.
Canada was the second country to adopt inflation targeting (after New Zealand).
The framework has been successful: inflation has averaged 2% since 1991.
The Bank publishes Monetary Policy Reports (quarterly) with economic projections.
The target is renewed every 5 years (last renewal 2021).
classifications.behavior:
The Bank of Canada's inflation-targeting framework is the cornerstone of its monetary policy. The target is 2% inflation (CPI), with a range of 1-3%. The Bank adjusts the overnight rate to keep inflation near the target. If inflation is above target, the Bank raises rates to cool the economy; if below target, it lowers rates to stimulate the economy.

Canada adopted inflation targeting in 1991, following New Zealand (1990). The framework was a response to the high inflation of the 1970s and 1980s. Since then, inflation has averaged 2%, and the economy has been more stable. The Bank's credibility has been enhanced by the framework.

Every 5 years, the Bank and the federal government renew the inflation-targeting agreement. The 2021 renewal reaffirmed the 2% target and added a requirement to consider maximum employment (similar to the Fed's dual mandate, but secondary).

The Bank publishes Monetary Policy Reports (MPR) quarterly, with economic projections (GDP, inflation) over a 2-3 year horizon. The MPR includes a fan chart showing the uncertainty around the inflation forecast. The Governor testifies before Parliament after each MPR.

For Canadians, inflation targeting means that the Bank is committed to keeping inflation low and stable. This anchors expectations: households and businesses can plan without worrying about high inflation or deflation.

image.alt: Graph: Canadian CPI inflation (1990-2024) with 2% target line; inflation has averaged near 2% since 1991.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.6: Canadian inflation since 1991. The Bank of Canada's inflation-targeting framework has successfully kept inflation near 2% for over three decades.

examples.explanation: In 2021-2022, Canadian inflation surged to 8% due to supply chain disruptions, energy prices, and fiscal stimulus. The Bank raised the overnight rate from 0.25% to 5.00% (2022-2023). Inflation fell to 3% by 2024. The Bank's credibility anchored expectations; long-term inflation expectations remained near 2%.

The Bank publishes the MPR quarterly. It includes economic projections for GDP and inflation, a fan chart for inflation (showing uncertainty), and a discussion of risks. The Governor holds a press conference to explain the report. This transparency enhances credibility.

principles.principle: The Corridor System Gives the Bank of Canada Precise Control Over the Overnight Rate
principles.statement: The Bank of Canada's corridor system provides a clear and predictable framework for implementing monetary policy. By setting the deposit rate (floor) and Bank Rate (ceiling) 25 basis points above and below the target, the Bank ensures that the overnight rate will trade within this band. The use of open market operations (SPRAs and LLAs) allows the Bank to fine-tune liquidity and keep the overnight rate very close to the target. This precision is important for transmitting monetary policy to the economy. The Fed's floor system also provides control, but the Fed relies on IORB rather than active OMOs. The corridor system requires more active management but gives the Bank more flexibility in responding to liquidity shocks. The system has worked well for Canada, contributing to low and stable inflation.
principles.application: The Bank of Canada should continue to use the corridor system, as it has proven effective. The Bank should maintain its active open market operations to keep the overnight rate near target. The Bank should also ensure that the deposit rate and Bank Rate are adjusted in sync with the target. For Canadians, the predictability of the overnight rate helps businesses and households make borrowing and investment decisions.
principles.example: During the 2020 pandemic, the Bank lowered the overnight rate target to 0.25%. The deposit rate was set at 0.00% (floor) and the Bank Rate at 0.50% (ceiling). The Bank conducted SPRAs and LLAs to keep the overnight rate near 0.25%. The corridor system functioned well despite the unprecedented shock.

image.alt: Graph: Overnight rate vs. target (2000-2024); very close tracking, demonstrating precision of corridor system.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.7: The Bank of Canada's corridor system keeps the overnight rate very close to the target. The precision of the system is evident in the tight tracking over time.

principles.principle: The Bank of Canada's Inflation Targeting Framework Has Been Highly Successful and Provides a Model for Other Central Banks
principles.statement: Canada's inflation-targeting framework (2% target since 1991) has been one of the most successful monetary policy frameworks in history. Inflation has averaged 2% for over three decades, and the economy has been more stable than before. The framework has anchored expectations, giving households and businesses confidence that inflation will remain low. The Bank's transparency (Monetary Policy Reports, press conferences, testimony) has enhanced credibility. The framework has been renewed every 5 years, with the 2021 renewal reaffirming the 2% target. Many other central banks (including the Fed in 2012) have adopted inflation targeting, following Canada's lead. The success of the framework is a testament to the Bank's independence, credibility, and skillful management.
principles.application: The Bank of Canada should maintain its inflation-targeting framework. The target should remain 2% (with a 1-3% range). The Bank should continue to publish Monetary Policy Reports and fan charts. The Bank should also consider the employment mandate (added in 2021) but keep inflation as the primary objective. For Canadians, the framework provides a stable environment for saving, borrowing, and investment.
principles.example: During the 2008 crisis, inflation fell below 1%. The Bank cut rates to 0.25% and provided forward guidance. Inflation returned to 2% by 2010. During the 2021-2022 inflation surge, the Bank raised rates aggressively. Inflation fell to 3% by 2024. The framework allowed the Bank to respond flexibly while maintaining credibility.

image.alt: Graph: Canadian inflation (1990-2024) vs. US inflation; both have been stable, but Canada's has been closer to target.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.8: Canadian inflation has been remarkably stable since the adoption of inflation targeting in 1991, averaging near 2% and providing a model for other central banks.

worked_examples.title: Calculating the Overnight Rate Band and Implementation

image.alt: Table: Overnight rate target 5.00%, deposit rate 4.75%, Bank Rate 5.25%; calculations of band and arbitrage.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.9: Worked example: calculating the overnight rate band and understanding how the corridor system keeps rates within the band.

worked_examples.problem:
The Bank of Canada sets the overnight rate target at 5.00%. The deposit rate is 4.75% (floor), and the Bank Rate is 5.25% (ceiling).

a) What is the width of the operating band?
b) If the overnight rate rises to 5.30%, what should banks do?
c) If the overnight rate falls to 4.70%, what should banks do?
d) The Bank conducts a SPRA (Sale and Repurchase Agreement) to add liquidity. Which direction does this push the overnight rate?
e) If the Bank wants to tighten policy by 25 basis points, how should it adjust the target, deposit rate, and Bank Rate?

step_by_step.explanation: Band width = Bank Rate - Deposit rate = 5.25% - 4.75% = 0.50 percentage points (50 basis points)

If the overnight rate rises to 5.30% (above the 5.25% ceiling), banks can borrow from the Bank of Canada at the Bank Rate (5.25%) and lend in the overnight market at 5.30%, earning a 5 basis point arbitrage profit. This arbitrage will push the overnight rate down to 5.25% or below.

In practice, banks may be reluctant to borrow from the Bank (stigma), so the Bank may conduct open market operations to add liquidity, pushing the rate down.

If the overnight rate falls to 4.70% (below the 4.75% floor), banks can borrow in the overnight market at 4.70% and deposit the funds at the Bank of Canada at 4.75%, earning a 5 basis point arbitrage profit. This arbitrage will push the overnight rate up to 4.75% or above.

If the rate remains below the floor, the Bank may conduct open market operations to drain liquidity, pushing the rate up.

A SPRA (Sale and Repurchase Agreement) is a repo operation where the Bank sells government bonds to primary dealers (draining reserves) with an agreement to repurchase them later. This drains liquidity from the banking system, which pushes the overnight rate up (since reserves become scarcer).

To tighten policy by 25 basis points, the Bank would raise the entire band by 25 basis points:

New overnight rate target = 5.00% + 0.25% = 5.25%
New deposit rate = 4.75% + 0.25% = 5.00%
New Bank Rate = 5.25% + 0.25% = 5.50%

The band width remains 50 basis points (5.00% to 5.50%).

worked_examples.final_answer: a) 50 basis points. b) Borrow from Bank at 5.25%, lend at 5.30% (arbitrage). c) Borrow at 4.70%, deposit at Bank at 4.75% (arbitrage). d) Pushes overnight rate up (drains liquidity). e) Raise all three by 25bp: target 5.25%, deposit 5.00%, Bank Rate 5.50%.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the corridor system mechanics to understand how the Bank of Canada keeps the overnight rate within the target band.

worked_examples.title: Comparing Bank of Canada and Federal Reserve Implementation Frameworks

image.alt: Table: BoC corridor (target 5.00%, deposit 4.75%, Bank Rate 5.25%) vs. Fed floor (IORB 5.40%, ON RRP 5.30%, target 5.25-5.50%).
image.caption: Figure 6.5.10: Worked example: comparing the Bank of Canada's corridor system with the Federal Reserve's floor system.

worked_examples.problem:
Compare the Bank of Canada's corridor system with the Federal Reserve's floor system.

Bank of Canada (Corridor):
• Overnight rate target: 5.00%
• Deposit rate (floor): 4.75%
• Bank Rate (ceiling): 5.25%

Federal Reserve (Floor):
• Target range: 5.25-5.50%
• IORB (floor for banks): 5.40%
• ON RRP (floor for non-banks): 5.30%
• Discount window (ceiling): 5.75%

a) What is the width of the BoC's operating band?
b) What is the width of the Fed's effective band (between ON RRP and discount window)?
c) How does the BoC keep the overnight rate near target?
d) How does the Fed keep the federal funds rate within target?
e) Which system requires more active open market operations? Why?

step_by_step.explanation: BoC band width = Bank Rate - Deposit rate = 5.25% - 4.75% = 50 basis points

Fed effective band = Discount window - ON RRP = 5.75% - 5.30% = 45 basis points

The federal funds rate typically trades between ON RRP (5.30%) and IORB (5.40%), a narrower band of 10 basis points. The discount window is a backstop rarely used.

The Bank of Canada uses active open market operations (SPRAs and LLAs) to keep the overnight rate near target. If the overnight rate rises above target, the Bank conducts a SPRA (adds reserves) to push it down. If it falls below target, the Bank conducts an LLA (drains reserves) to push it up. The Bank also relies on the corridor (deposit rate floor, Bank Rate ceiling) to bound the rate.

The Federal Reserve uses a floor system. IORB (interest on reserves) acts as a floor: banks will not lend reserves below IORB. ON RRP provides a floor for non-banks. The discount window acts as a ceiling (rarely used). The Fed does not need to conduct active open market operations because the floors do most of the work. The Fed sets IORB and ON RRP, and the market determines the federal funds rate within the band.

step_by_step.step: Which system requires more active OMOs?
step_by_step.explanation:
The Bank of Canada's corridor system requires more active open market operations because the Bank must fine-tune liquidity daily to keep the overnight rate near target. The Fed's floor system is more passive: once IORB and ON RRP are set, the federal funds rate stays within the band without active OMOs. However, the Fed may still conduct repos (e.g., during year-end stress) to manage liquidity.

The BoC's corridor system historically operated with scarce reserves, requiring daily OMOs. After QE (2020-2022), reserves became ample, reducing the need for active OMOs, but the BoC still conducts more active operations than the Fed.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) 50bp. b) 45bp (effective 10bp between ON RRP and IORB). c) Active OMOs (SPRAs, LLAs). d) IORB and ON RRP floors. e) BoC requires more active OMOs (fine-tuning); Fed is more passive (floor system).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Comparing the Bank of Canada's corridor system with the Federal Reserve's floor system to understand different approaches to monetary policy implementation.

process_flow.process: Governing Council sets overnight rate target (8 times per year)
process_flow.description: The Governing Council meets 8 times per year to review economic projections and set the overnight rate target.
process_flow.mechanism: MPR forecasts; discussion of inflation risks; consensus decision; announcement at 10:00 AM ET.

image.alt: Diagram: Governing Council meeting → MPR forecasts → policy decision → overnight rate target.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.11: Step 1 - The Governing Council meets 8 times per year to set the overnight rate target based on economic projections.

process_flow.process: Bank sets deposit rate and Bank Rate (corridor)
process_flow.description: The Bank sets the deposit rate (floor) 25bp below the target and the Bank Rate (ceiling) 25bp above the target.
process_flow.mechanism: Deposit rate = target - 0.25%; Bank Rate = target + 0.25%.

image.alt: Equation: Deposit rate = target - 0.25%, Bank Rate = target + 0.25%.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.12: Step 2 - The Bank sets the deposit rate (floor) and Bank Rate (ceiling) 25 basis points above and below the target.

process_flow.process: Bank conducts open market operations (SPRAs, LLAs) to keep overnight rate near target
process_flow.description: The Bank uses SPRAs (add liquidity) and LLAs (drain liquidity) to keep the overnight rate within the corridor.
process_flow.mechanism: If overnight rate > target → SPRA (add reserves); if overnight rate < target → LLA (drain reserves).

image.alt: Decision tree: Overnight rate > target → SPRA; overnight rate < target → LLA.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.13: Step 3 - The Bank conducts open market operations (SPRAs and LLAs) to keep the overnight rate near the target.

process_flow.process: Overnight rate transmits to other interest rates (prime rate, mortgages, bonds)
process_flow.description: Changes in the overnight rate affect the prime rate (target + 2.2%), mortgage rates, and other lending rates.
process_flow.mechanism: Prime rate = overnight rate + 2.2%; mortgage rates = prime rate + spread.

image.alt: Flowchart: Overnight rate → prime rate → mortgage rates, business loans → consumption, investment → inflation.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.14: Step 4 - The overnight rate transmits to other interest rates and ultimately to inflation and output.

process_flow.process: Bank monitors inflation and adjusts policy as needed
process_flow.description: The Bank monitors CPI inflation and adjusts the overnight rate to keep inflation near the 2% target.
process_flow.mechanism: If inflation > 2% (and persistent) → raise rates; if inflation < 2% → lower rates.

image.alt: Decision tree: Inflation > 2% → raise rates; inflation < 2% → lower rates.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.15: Step 5 - The Bank monitors inflation and adjusts the overnight rate to maintain the 2% target.

image.alt: Master framework: Governing Council sets target → corridor (deposit rate, Bank Rate) → open market operations (SPRAs, LLAs) → overnight rate → transmission to economy → inflation monitoring → policy adjustment.
image.caption: Figure 6.5.16: Complete framework for the Bank of Canada's monetary policy implementation.

key_terms: Comparison: Bank of Canada vs. Federal Reserve
summary: The Bank of Canada is Canada's central bank, responsible for monetary policy, financial stability, currency issuance, and payment system oversight. The Bank's governance structure separates monetary policy (Governing Council: Governor, Senior Deputy Governor, 4 Deputy Governors) from administration (Board of Directors). The Bank operates a corridor system for implementing monetary policy: the overnight rate target is the midpoint of a 50 basis point band (deposit rate floor, Bank Rate ceiling). The Bank uses open market operations (SPRAs to add liquidity, LLAs to drain liquidity) to keep the overnight rate near the target. This contrasts with the Federal Reserve's floor system (IORB floor, ON RRP floor). The Bank has a single mandate: price stability (2% inflation target since 1991), making it one of the most successful inflation-targeting central banks. The Bank publishes Monetary Policy Reports quarterly and the Governor testifies before Parliament twice per year, ensuring accountability. The Bank also acts as lender of last resort, manages foreign exchange reserves, and operates Canada's large-value payment systems (Lynx, LVTS). Understanding the Bank of Canada's structure is essential for analyzing Canadian monetary policy, comparing it to other central banks, and evaluating the effectiveness of the corridor system and inflation targeting.
